entry.title: LeetCode做题记录
entry.content:
2026.4.25

3464. 正方形上的点之间的最大距离

min-max问题，考虑到正方形边界点曼哈顿距离上界为 [4×sidek\frac{4 \times \text{side}}{k}k4×side​]，可以直接对其二分答案。同时注意到在相邻边或者同一条边上的点曼哈顿距离等价于直接把整个正方形拉成一条边来算，而对边上点的曼哈顿距离显然超出上界可以直接忽略。

我们对每个点预处理出在一条线上时候的distdistdist，二分答案，枚举第一个出现的点，每次使用二分查找第一个超过上界的点，如此下去，即可贪心地选取kkk个点。 代码如下：

entry.title: 统计学习方法 Notes: Lagrange Duality
entry.content:
Lagrange Duality is an useful tool which could be used in SVM(Machine Learning) and some algorithms.

In constrained optimization, Lagrange duality is frequently used to transform a primal problem into a dual problem. By solving the dual, we can often find the solution to the primal more easily.

The Primal Problem

Consider a constrained optimization problem where f(x)f(x)f(x), ci(x)c_i(x)ci​(x), and hj(x)h_j(x)hj​(x) are continuous, differentiable functions defined on Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn.

The primal optimization problem is defined as:

min⁡x∈Rnf(x)\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(x)x∈Rnmin​f(x)

s.t.ci(x)≤0,i=1,2,…,ks.t. \quad c_i(x) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, ks.t.ci​(x)≤0,i=1,2,…,k

hj(x)=0,j=1,2,…,lh_j(x) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, lhj​(x)=0,j=1,2,…,l

To solve this, we first introduce the generalized Lagrange function:

L(x,α,β)=f(x)+∑i=1kαici(x)+∑j=1lβjhj(x)L(x, \alpha, \beta) = f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^k \alpha_i c_i(x) + \sum_{j=1}^l \beta_j h_j(x)L(x,α,β)=f(x)+i=1∑k​αi​ci​(x)+j=1∑l​βj​hj​(x)

where:

  • x=(x(1),x(2),…,x(n))T∈Rnx = (x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \dots, x^{(n)})^T \in \mathbb{R}^nx=(x(1),x(2),…,x(n))T∈Rn

  • Lagrange Multipliers are αi\alpha_iαi​ and βj\beta_jβj​, where we strictly require αi≥0\alpha_i \ge 0αi​≥0 for the inequality constraints.

Next, we define a function of xxx called the primal objective function, denoted as θP(x)\theta_P(x)θP​(x):

θP(x)=max⁡α,β:αi≥0L(x,α,β)\theta_P(x) = \max_{\alpha, \beta: \alpha_i \ge 0} L(x, \alpha, \beta)θP​(x)=α,β:αi​≥0max​L(x,α,β)

Then we could give a brief analysis on the θP(x)\theta_P(x)θP​(x):

  • If xxx violates any constraint, we can let αi→+∞\alpha_i \to +\inftyαi​→+∞ or βj\beta_jβj​ be a very large value (positive or negative) such that βjhj(x)→+∞\beta_j h_j(x) \to +\inftyβj​hj​(x)→+∞. In all these cases θP(x)=+∞\theta_P(x) = +\inftyθP​(x)=+∞.

  • If xxx satisfies all constraints, since ci(x)≤0c_i(x) \le 0ci​(x)≤0 and αi≥0\alpha_i \ge 0αi​≥0, the term αici(x)\alpha_i c_i(x)αi​ci​(x) is at most 000. To maximize the sum, we set αi=0\alpha_i = 0αi​=0 (if ci(x)<0c_i(x) < 0ci​(x)<0) or it remains 000 (if ci(x)=0c_i(x) = 0ci​(x)=0).In this case, θP(x)=f(x)\theta_P(x) = f(x)θP​(x)=f(x).

Thus, the function is simplified to:

θP(x)={f(x),if x satisfies all primal constraints+∞,otherwise\theta_P(x) = \begin{cases} f(x), & \text{if } x \text{ satisfies all primal constraints} \\+\infty, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}θP​(x)={f(x),+∞,​if x satisfies all primal constraintsotherwise​

Therefore, the problem min⁡xθP(x)\min_x \theta_P(x)minx​θP​(x) is equivalent to the original primal problem. We define the optimal value of the primal problem as:

p∗=min⁡xθP(x)=min⁡xmax⁡α,β:αi≥0L(x,α,β)p^* = \min_x \theta_P(x) = \min_x \max_{\alpha, \beta: \alpha_i \ge 0} L(x, \alpha, \beta)p∗=xmin​θP​(x)=xmin​α,β:αi​≥0max​L(x,α,β)

The Dual Problem

The dual problem is created by flipping the order of the “min” and “max” operations.

First, we define the dual objective function:

θD(α,β)=min⁡xL(x,α,β)\theta_D(\alpha, \beta) = \min_x L(x, \alpha, \beta)θD​(α,β)=xmin​L(x,α,β)

Then, we seek to maximize this function with respect to the multipliers α\alphaα and β\betaβ (subject to αi≥0\alpha_i \ge 0αi​≥0):

max⁡α,β:αi≥0θD(α,β)=max⁡α,β:αi≥0min⁡xL(x,α,β)\max_{\alpha, \beta: \alpha_i \ge 0} \theta_D(\alpha, \beta) = \max_{\alpha, \beta: \alpha_i \ge 0} \min_x L(x, \alpha, \beta)α,β:αi​≥0max​θD​(α,β)=α,β:αi​≥0max​xmin​L(x,α,β)

This is known as the max-min problem. We define the optimal value of the dual problem as d∗d^*d∗:

d∗=max⁡α,β:αi≥0θD(α,β)d^* = \max_{\alpha, \beta: \alpha_i \ge 0} \theta_D(\alpha, \beta)d∗=α,β:αi​≥0max​θD​(α,β)

Relationship Between Primal and Dual Problems

Theorem 1 Weak Duality:
If both the primal and dual problems have optimal values, then the dual optimal value d∗d^*d∗ is always less than or equal to the primal optimal value p∗p^*p∗:

d∗=max⁡α,β:αi≥0min⁡xL(x,α,β)≤min⁡xmax⁡α,β:αi≥0L(x,α,β)=p∗d^* = \max_{\alpha, \beta: \alpha_i \ge 0} \min_x L(x, \alpha, \beta) \le \min_x \max_{\alpha, \beta: \alpha_i \ge 0} L(x, \alpha, \beta) = p^*d∗=α,β:αi​≥0max​xmin​L(x,α,β)≤xmin​α,β:αi​≥0max​L(x,α,β)=p∗

Proof:∀x,α,β\forall x, \alpha, \beta∀x,α,β, the following inequality holds by definition:

θD(α,β)=min⁡xL(x,α,β)≤L(x,α,β)≤max⁡α,β:αi≥0L(x,α,β)=θP(x)\theta_D(\alpha, \beta) = \min_x L(x, \alpha, \beta) \le L(x, \alpha, \beta) \le \max_{\alpha, \beta: \alpha_i \ge 0} L(x, \alpha, \beta) = \theta_P(x)θD​(α,β)=xmin​L(x,α,β)≤L(x,α,β)≤α,β:αi​≥0max​L(x,α,β)=θP​(x)

Since θD(α,β)≤θP(x)\theta_D(\alpha, \beta) \le \theta_P(x)θD​(α,β)≤θP​(x) for all variables, it follows that the maximum of the lower bound (θD\theta_DθD​) cannot exceed the minimum of the upper bound (θP\theta_PθP​).

Theorem 2 Strong Duality:
If f(x)f(x)f(x) and ci(x)c_i(x)ci​(x) are convex functions, hj(x)h_j(x)hj​(x) are affine functions (hj(x)=ajTx+bjh_j(x) = a_j^T x + b_jhj​(x)=ajT​x+bj​), and the inequality constraints are strictly feasible (Slater’s condition: ∃x\exists x∃x such that ci(x)<0c_i(x) < 0ci​(x)<0 for all iii), then there exist x∗,α∗,β∗x^*, \alpha^*, \beta^*x∗,α∗,β∗ such that x∗x^*x∗ is the primal solution, α∗,β∗\alpha^*, \beta^*α∗,β∗ are the dual solutions, and:

p∗=d∗=L(x∗,α∗,β∗)p^* = d^* = L(x^*, \alpha^*, \beta^*)p∗=d∗=L(x∗,α∗,β∗)

Proof:

Lemma 1 Hyperplane Separation Theorem:
Let CCC and DDD be two non-empty disjoint convex sets in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn (i.e., C∩D=∅C \cap D = \emptysetC∩D=∅). Then there exists a non-zero vector a∈Rna \in \mathbb{R}^na∈Rn and a scalar b∈Rb \in \mathbb{R}b∈R such that:

aTx≤bfor all x∈Ca^T x \le b \quad \text{for all } x \in CaTx≤bfor all x∈C

and

aTy≥bfor all y∈Da^T y \ge b \quad \text{for all } y \in DaTy≥bfor all y∈D

The equation aTx=ba^T x = baTx=b defines a hyperplane that separates the two sets. The vector aaa is the normal vector to the hyperplane.

Lemma 2 Supporting Hyperplane Theorem:

Let CCC be a non-empty convex set in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn, and let x0x_0x0​ be a point on the boundary of CCC (i.e., x0∈∂Cx_0 \in \partial Cx0​∈∂C). Then there exists a non-zero vector a∈Rna \in \mathbb{R}^na∈Rn such that:

aTx≤aTx0for all x∈Ca^T x \le a^T x_0 \quad \text{for all } x \in CaTx≤aTx0​for all x∈C

Let G={(c1(x),…,ck(x),h1(x),…,hl(x),f(x))∈Rk+l+1∣x∈Rn}\mathcal{G} = \{ (c_1(x), \dots, c_k(x), h_1(x), \dots, h_l(x), f(x)) \in \mathbb{R}^{k+l+1} \mid x \in \mathbb{R}^n \}G={(c1​(x),…,ck​(x),h1​(x),…,hl​(x),f(x))∈Rk+l+1∣x∈Rn}.

Because fff and cic_ici​ are convex and hjh_jhj​ is affine, we can define a convex set A\mathcal{A}A that “sits above” G\mathcal{G}G.

According to the lemma 2, there exists a hyperplane that separates the set of unattainable values (values below p∗p^*p∗) from the set of attainable values A\mathcal{A}A. And this hyperplane defines the optimal Lagrange multipliers α∗\alpha^*α∗ and β∗\beta^*β∗.

Slater’s condition ensures that the hyperplane is not “vertical” (i.e., the coefficient associated with f(x)f(x)f(x) is non-zero).

The equation of this hyperplane in the (u,v,t)(u, v, t)(u,v,t) space is:

t+∑i=1kαi∗ui+∑j=1lβj∗vj≥ct + \sum_{i=1}^k \alpha_i^* u_i + \sum_{j=1}^l \beta_j^* v_j \ge ct+i=1∑k​αi∗​ui​+j=1∑l​βj∗​vj​≥c

Since the hyperplane passes through the boundary point (0,0,p∗)(0, 0, p^*)(0,0,p∗), we substitute these coordinates to find the constant ccc:

p∗+∑αi∗(0)+∑βj∗(0)=c  ⟹  c=p∗p^* + \sum \alpha_i^*(0) + \sum \beta_j^*(0) = c \implies c = p^*p∗+∑αi∗​(0)+∑βj∗​(0)=c⟹c=p∗

Thus, for any point (u,v,t)∈A(u, v, t) \in \mathcal{A}(u,v,t)∈A, the following inequality holds:

t+∑i=1kαi∗ui+∑j=1lβj∗vj≥p∗t + \sum_{i=1}^k \alpha_i^* u_i + \sum_{j=1}^l \beta_j^* v_j \ge p^*t+i=1∑k​αi∗​ui​+j=1∑l​βj∗​vj​≥p∗

By the definition of set A\mathcal{A}A, for any x∈Rnx \in \mathbb{R}^nx∈Rn, the point (c(x),h(x),f(x))(c(x), h(x), f(x))(c(x),h(x),f(x)) is an element of A\mathcal{A}A.

Substituting these into the hyperplane equation gives us:

f(x)+∑i=1kαi∗ci(x)+∑j=1lβj∗hj(x)≥p∗f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^k \alpha_i^* c_i(x) + \sum_{j=1}^l \beta_j^* h_j(x) \ge p^*f(x)+i=1∑k​αi∗​ci​(x)+j=1∑l​βj∗​hj​(x)≥p∗

The left-hand side is exactly the Generalized Lagrangian Function L(x,α∗,β∗)L(x, \alpha^*, \beta^*)L(x,α∗,β∗). Therefore:

L(x,α∗,β∗)≥p∗∀x∈RnL(x, \alpha^*, \beta^*) \ge p^* \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}^nL(x,α∗,β∗)≥p∗∀x∈Rn

The dual function θD(α,β)\theta_D(\alpha, \beta)θD​(α,β) is defined as the infimum of the Lagrangian over xxx. Since the inequality above holds for all xxx, it must also hold for the minimum:

θD(α∗,β∗)=inf⁡xL(x,α∗,β∗)≥p∗\theta_D(\alpha^*, \beta^*) = \inf_{x} L(x, \alpha^*, \beta^*) \ge p^*θD​(α∗,β∗)=xinf​L(x,α∗,β∗)≥p∗

By definition, the optimal dual value d∗d^*d∗ is the maximum of θD(α,β)\theta_D(\alpha, \beta)θD​(α,β). Since we have found a specific pair (α∗,β∗)(\alpha^*, \beta^*)(α∗,β∗) that yields a value ≥p∗\ge p^*≥p∗, we conclude:

d∗=max⁡α,βθD(α,β)≥θD(α∗,β∗)≥p∗d^* = \max_{\alpha, \beta} \theta_D(\alpha, \beta) \ge \theta_D(\alpha^*, \beta^*) \ge p^*d∗=α,βmax​θD​(α,β)≥θD​(α∗,β∗)≥p∗

Which gives us:

d∗≥p∗d^* \ge p^*d∗≥p∗

We also have the Weak Duality property, then:

d∗=p∗d^* = p^*d∗=p∗

Theorem 3 KKT Conditions:
Given the same convexity and feasibility assumptions as Theorem 2, the points x∗,α∗,β∗x^*, \alpha^*, \beta^*x∗,α∗,β∗ are the optimal solutions to the primal and dual problems if and only if they satisfy the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions:

  • ∇xL(x∗,α∗,β∗)=0\nabla_x L(x^*, \alpha^*, \beta^*) = 0∇x​L(x∗,α∗,β∗)=0 (Stationarity)

  • αi∗ci(x∗)=0,i=1,…,k\alpha_i^* c_i(x^*) = 0, \quad i=1, \dots, kαi∗​ci​(x∗)=0,i=1,…,k (Complementary Slackness)

  • ci(x∗)≤0,i=1,…,kc_i(x^*) \le 0, \quad i=1, \dots, kci​(x∗)≤0,i=1,…,k (Primal Feasibility)

  • αi∗≥0,i=1,…,k\alpha_i^* \ge 0, \quad i=1, \dots, kαi∗​≥0,i=1,…,k (Dual Feasibility)

  • hj(x∗)=0,j=1,…,lh_j(x^*) = 0, \quad j=1, \dots, lhj​(x∗)=0,j=1,…,l (Primal Feasibility)

Proof:

Necessity:

Since x∗x^*x∗ minimizes L(x,α∗,β∗)L(x, \alpha^*, \beta^*)L(x,α∗,β∗) (from the definition of the dual), its gradient must be zero: ∇xL=0\nabla_x L = 0∇x​L=0.

We know p∗=d∗p^* = d^*p∗=d∗, which implies:

f(x∗)=θD(α∗,β∗)=min⁡xL(x,α∗,β∗)≤L(x∗,α∗,β∗)f(x^*) = \theta_D(\alpha^*, \beta^*) = \min_x L(x, \alpha^*, \beta^*) \le L(x^*, \alpha^*, \beta^*)f(x∗)=θD​(α∗,β∗)=xmin​L(x,α∗,β∗)≤L(x∗,α∗,β∗)

f(x∗)≤f(x∗)+∑αi∗ci(x∗)+∑βj∗hj(x∗)f(x^*) \le f(x^*) + \sum \alpha_i^* c_i(x^*) + \sum \beta_j^* h_j(x^*)f(x∗)≤f(x∗)+∑αi∗​ci​(x∗)+∑βj∗​hj​(x∗)

Since hj(x∗)=0h_j(x^*) = 0hj​(x∗)=0 and αi∗≥0,ci(x∗)≤0\alpha_i^* \ge 0, c_i(x^*) \le 0αi∗​≥0,ci​(x∗)≤0, the summation ∑αi∗ci(x∗)\sum \alpha_i^* c_i(x^*)∑αi∗​ci​(x∗) must be ≤0\le 0≤0.

For the inequality f(x∗)≤f(x∗)+non-positive termf(x^*) \le f(x^*) + \text{non-positive term}f(x∗)≤f(x∗)+non-positive term to hold as an equality, we must have ∑αi∗ci(x∗)=0\sum \alpha_i^* c_i(x^*) = 0∑αi∗​ci​(x∗)=0. Since each term is ≤0\le 0≤0, each individual term αi∗ci(x∗)\alpha_i^* c_i(x^*)αi∗​ci​(x∗) must be zero.

Sufficiency:

From KKT-1, x∗x^*x∗ is a global minimum of L(x,α∗,β∗)L(x, \alpha^*, \beta^*)L(x,α∗,β∗) because LLL is convex in xxx. Therefore, θD(α∗,β∗)=L(x∗,α∗,β∗)\theta_D(\alpha^*, \beta^*) = L(x^*, \alpha^*, \beta^*)θD​(α∗,β∗)=L(x∗,α∗,β∗).

Using KKT-2 (slackness) and KKT-5 (equality constraints):

L(x∗,α∗,β∗)=f(x∗)+0+0=f(x∗)L(x^*, \alpha^*, \beta^*) = f(x^*) + 0 + 0 = f(x^*)L(x∗,α∗,β∗)=f(x∗)+0+0=f(x∗)

Thus, θD(α∗,β∗)=f(x∗)\theta_D(\alpha^*, \beta^*) = f(x^*)θD​(α∗,β∗)=f(x∗), which means d∗=p∗d^* = p^*d∗=p∗.

By the Duality Theorem, x∗x^*x∗ and (α∗,β∗)(\alpha^*, \beta^*)(α∗,β∗) must be the primal and dual solutions respectively.

entry.title: Data_Structure 1: Disjoint Set(Union Find)
entry.content:
From this article, I will introduce some easy data structures in that category. The first one is Union Find.

Why we develop this data structure

The genesis of Union-Find stems from the need to solve the Equivalence Problem in computer science. In early computational theory, researchers were looking for an efficient way to process a series of “equivalence relations” (e.g., a≡ba \equiv ba≡b, b≡cb \equiv cb≡c) and determine if two elements belong to the same equivalence class.

In the classic paper “An Improved Equivalence Algorithm,”[1] the core motivation was to move beyond simple linked lists or arrays, which were computationally expensive for large datasets. Before Union-Find, checking if two elements were connected often required traversing the entire structure, leading to O(N)O(N)O(N) time complexity for a single query.

Here is some examples before Union-Find:

Algorithm 1: Naive Approach
The most intuitive way to track set membership is through a simple mapping.

Data Representation: We maintain an array A[1...n]A[1...n]A[1...n], where the index represents the element and the value stored at A[x]A[x]A[x] is the “name” (or ID) of the set to which xxx belongs. At the start of the algorithm, we initialize each element to be its own parent: A[x]=xA[x] = xA[x]=x for all xxx.

Find(x)Find(x)Find(x):Find(x)Find(x)Find(x) simply returns A[x]A[x]A[x], which runs in O(1)O(1)O(1) time.

Union(x,y)Union(x, y)Union(x,y):Union(x,y)Union(x, y)Union(x,y) first checks if A[x]A[x]A[x] and A[y]A[y]A[y] are the same. If they are different, it iterates through the entire array and updates all entries that match A[y]A[y]A[y] to A[x]A[x]A[x]. This operation runs in O(n)O(n)O(n) time in the worst case.

Algorithm 2: The Linked-List Representation
we can represent each set as a linked list. In this model, our data structure is essentially a collection of disjoint lists, where each node represents an element.

Find(x)Find(x)Find(x):Find(x)Find(x)Find(x) traverses the linked list to find the representative (or “root”) of the set that contains xxx. This operation can take O(n)O(n)O(n) time in the worst case if the list is long.

Union(x,y)Union(x, y)Union(x,y):Union(x,y)Union(x, y)Union(x,y) merges the two linked lists corresponding to the sets of xxx and yyy. This involves finding the tail of one list and linking it to the head of the other, which can also take O(n)O(n)O(n) time in the worst case.

Algorithm 3: An approach to optimize Algorithm 2
To optimize the linked-list representation, we can put a pointer of every node to the head of the list. This way, we can find the representative of any element in O(1)O(1)O(1) time.

Find(x)Find(x)Find(x):Find(x)Find(x)Find(x) simply returns the head pointer of the node representing xxx, which runs in O(1)O(1)O(1) time.

Union(x,y)Union(x, y)Union(x,y):Union(x,y)Union(x, y)Union(x,y) merges the two linked lists corresponding to the sets of xxx and yyy. This involves finding the tail of one list and linking it to the head of the other, which can still take O(n)O(n)O(n) time in the worst case.

The Union-Find Data Structure

The Union-Find data structure, also known as Disjoint Set Union (DSU), is designed to efficiently manage a collection of disjoint sets. It provides two primary operations: Find and Union.

We get the idea from the Algorithm 2 to use a set of trees(or forests) to represent the sets. Each tree represents a set, and the root of the tree is the representative (or “leader”) of that set. Each node in the tree points to its parent, and the root node points to itself.

Path Compression

One optimization technique for Union-Find is path compression which have appeared in Algorithm 3. During a Find operation, we can make every node on the path from the queried node to the root point directly to the root. This significantly speeds up future queries.

Here is an example:

Union by Rank

Another optimization is union by rank. If we simply perform a union by making one root point to another, we might accidentally create a very deep tree (in the worst case, a degenerate tree that looks like a linked list).

Instead of randomly picking which tree becomes the parent, we track a “rank” for each node. Initially, every node starts with a rank of 0. When it comes time to merge two sets:

1.We compare the ranks of the two roots.

2.The root with the lower rank is attached under the root with the higher rank.

3.If both roots have the same rank, we pick one to be the parent and increment its rank by 1.

The reason why we don’t maintain the height is that once we apply Path Compression, the actual height of the tree changes constantly. “Rank” serves as an upper bound on the height of the tree and remains a stable metric for making merging decisions.

Here is an example:

When both Path Compression and Union by Rank/Size are used, the amortized time complexity per operation is:O(α(n))O(\alpha(n))O(α(n))

Where:

nnn is the number of elements.

α(n)\alpha(n)α(n) is the Inverse Ackermann Function which is an extremely slow-growing function.

We can use amortized analysis to prove the property. You can refer to the paper by Tarjan [2].

…

  1. Galler, B. A., & Fischer, M. J. (1964). An improved equivalence algorithm. ↩
  2. Tarjan, R. E. (1975). Efficiency of a Good But Not Quite Linear Disjoint Set Union Algorithm. Journal of the ACM (JACM), 22(2), 215-225. ↩

entry.title: CS229 Problem Set 1: Supervised Learning
entry.content:
1. [40 points] Linear Classifiers (logistic regression and GDA)

(a) [10 points]

We suppose the hypothesis function is defined as:

hθ(x)=g(θTx)=11+e−θTxh_\theta(x) = g(\theta^T x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}hθ​(x)=g(θTx)=1+e−θTx1​

where g(z)=11+e−zg(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}g(z)=1+e−z1​ is the sigmoid (logistic) function.

The likelihood function for mmm independent observations is:

L(θ)=∏i=1m(hθ(x(i)))y(i)(1−hθ(x(i)))1−y(i)L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^m \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) \right)^{y^{(i)}} \left( 1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)}) \right)^{1-y^{(i)}}L(θ)=i=1∏m​(hθ​(x(i)))y(i)(1−hθ​(x(i)))1−y(i)

The log-likelihood function is:

ℓ(θ)=∑i=1m[y(i)log⁡hθ(x(i))+(1−y(i))log⁡(1−hθ(x(i)))]\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^m \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]ℓ(θ)=i=1∑m​[y(i)loghθ​(x(i))+(1−y(i))log(1−hθ​(x(i)))]

To find the optimal parameters, we need to maximize ℓ(θ)\ell(\theta)ℓ(θ). This is equivalent to minimizing the average empirical loss J(θ)J(\theta)J(θ):

J(θ)=−1m∑i=1m[y(i)log⁡hθ(x(i))+(1−y(i))log⁡(1−hθ(x(i)))]J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]J(θ)=−m1​i=1∑m​[y(i)loghθ​(x(i))+(1−y(i))log(1−hθ​(x(i)))]

where y(i)∈{0,1}y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}y(i)∈{0,1}.

To find the gradient, we first notice the property of the sigmoid function:

For a single sample (i)(i)(i), the partial derivative with respect to θj\theta_jθj​ is:

=−[y(i)−y(i)g(θTx(i))−g(θTx(i))+y(i)g(θTx(i))g(θTx(i))(1−g(θTx(i)))]g(θTx(i))(1−g(θTx(i)))xj(i)= -\left[ \frac{y^{(i)} - y^{(i)}g(\theta^T x^{(i)}) - g(\theta^T x^{(i)}) + y^{(i)}g(\theta^T x^{(i)})}{g(\theta^T x^{(i)})(1 - g(\theta^T x^{(i)}))} \right] g(\theta^T x^{(i)})(1 - g(\theta^T x^{(i)})) x_j^{(i)}=−[g(θTx(i))(1−g(θTx(i)))y(i)−y(i)g(θTx(i))−g(θTx(i))+y(i)g(θTx(i))​]g(θTx(i))(1−g(θTx(i)))xj(i)​

=−[y(i)−g(θTx(i))]xj(i)= -\left[ y^{(i)} - g(\theta^T x^{(i)}) \right] x_j^{(i)}=−[y(i)−g(θTx(i))]xj(i)​

=[hθ(x(i))−y(i)]xj(i)= \left[ h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right] x_j^{(i)}=[hθ​(x(i))−y(i)]xj(i)​

Summing over all mmm samples, the total gradient is:

∂J(θ)∂θj=1m∑i=1m(hθ(x(i))−y(i))xj(i)\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}∂θj​∂J(θ)​=m1​i=1∑m​(hθ​(x(i))−y(i))xj(i)​

In vector form:

To find the Hessian matrix HHH, we take the second derivative:

=1m∑i=1mxj(i)∂g(θTx(i))∂θk= \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^m x_j^{(i)} \frac{\partial g(\theta^T x^{(i)})}{\partial \theta_k}=m1​i=1∑m​xj(i)​∂θk​∂g(θTx(i))​

2. [30 points] Incomplete, Positive-Only Labels

We consider a binary classification task where t∈{0,1}t \in \{0, 1\}t∈{0,1} is the true label and y∈{0,1}y \in \{0, 1\}y∈{0,1} is the observed label.

p(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1,x(i))=p(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1)=αp(y^{(i)}=1 | t^{(i)}=1, x^{(i)}) = p(y^{(i)}=1 | t^{(i)}=1) = \alphap(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1,x(i))=p(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1)=α

Our goal is to prove that:

p(t(i)=1∣x(i))=p(y(i)=1∣x(i))αp(t^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)}) = \frac{p(y^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)})}{\alpha}p(t(i)=1∣x(i))=αp(y(i)=1∣x(i))​

(a) [5 points]

We start by expanding the probability of an example being labeled (y=1y=1y=1) given its features xxx:

p(y(i)=1∣x(i))=p(y(i)=1,t(i)=1∣x(i))+p(y(i)=1,t(i)=0∣x(i))p(y^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)}) = p(y^{(i)}=1, t^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)}) + p(y^{(i)}=1, t^{(i)}=0 | x^{(i)})p(y(i)=1∣x(i))=p(y(i)=1,t(i)=1∣x(i))+p(y(i)=1,t(i)=0∣x(i))

Since an example can only be labeled if it is positive (y=1  ⟹  t=1y=1 \implies t=1y=1⟹t=1), the term p(y(i)=1,t(i)=0∣x(i))p(y^{(i)}=1, t^{(i)}=0 | x^{(i)})p(y(i)=1,t(i)=0∣x(i)) must be 000. Thus:

p(y(i)=1∣x(i))=p(y(i)=1,t(i)=1∣x(i))p(y^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)}) = p(y^{(i)}=1, t^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)})p(y(i)=1∣x(i))=p(y(i)=1,t(i)=1∣x(i))

Using the definition of conditional probability:

p(y(i)=1∣x(i))=p(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1,x(i))⋅p(t(i)=1∣x(i))p(y^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)}) = p(y^{(i)}=1 | t^{(i)}=1, x^{(i)}) \cdot p(t^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)})p(y(i)=1∣x(i))=p(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1,x(i))⋅p(t(i)=1∣x(i))

Now, we apply the crucial assumption of conditional independence:

p(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1,x(i))=p(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1)p(y^{(i)}=1 | t^{(i)}=1, x^{(i)}) = p(y^{(i)}=1 | t^{(i)}=1)p(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1,x(i))=p(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1)

Let this constant probability be α\alphaα:

α=p(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1)\alpha = p(y^{(i)}=1 | t^{(i)}=1)α=p(y(i)=1∣t(i)=1)

Substituting α\alphaα back into the previous equation:

p(y(i)=1∣x(i))=α⋅p(t(i)=1∣x(i))p(y^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)}) = \alpha \cdot p(t^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)})p(y(i)=1∣x(i))=α⋅p(t(i)=1∣x(i))

Thus:

p(t(i)=1∣x(i))=p(y(i)=1∣x(i))αp(t^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)}) = \frac{p(y^{(i)}=1 | x^{(i)})}{\alpha}p(t(i)=1∣x(i))=αp(y(i)=1∣x(i))​

This concludes the proof that the true probability p(t=1∣x)p(t=1|x)p(t=1∣x) differs from the observed probability p(y=1∣x)p(y=1|x)p(y=1∣x) by a constant factor α\alphaα, which represents the fraction of positive examples that are labeled.

(b) [5 points]

Given p(t(i)=1∣x(i))≈1p(t^{(i)} = 1|x^{(i)}) \approx 1p(t(i)=1∣x(i))≈1 when x(i)∈V+x^{(i)} \in V_+x(i)∈V+​,

h(x(i))=p(y(i)=1∣x(i))=α⋅p(t(i)=1∣x(i))≈αh(x^{(i)}) = p(y^{(i)}=1|x^{(i)}) = \alpha \cdot p(t^{(i)}=1|x^{(i)}) \approx \alphah(x(i))=p(y(i)=1∣x(i))=α⋅p(t(i)=1∣x(i))≈α

3. [25 points] Poisson Regression

(a) [5 points]

The probability mass function (PMF) of a Poisson distribution is:

p(y;λ)=e−λλyy!p(y; \lambda) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^y}{y!}p(y;λ)=y!e−λλy​

where λ>0\lambda > 0λ>0 is the rate parameter.

Review: Exponential Family

A distribution belongs to the exponential family if it can be written in the form:

p(y;η)=b(y)exp⁡(ηTT(y)−a(η))p(y; \eta) = b(y) \exp\left( \eta^T T(y) - a(\eta) \right)p(y;η)=b(y)exp(ηTT(y)−a(η))

where:

  • η\etaη: natural parameter
  • a(η)a(\eta)a(η): log-partition function

We can rewrite the Poisson PMF as:

p(y;λ)=1y!exp⁡(−λ+yln⁡λ)p(y; \lambda) = \frac{1}{y!} \exp\left( -\lambda + y \ln \lambda \right)p(y;λ)=y!1​exp(−λ+ylnλ)

By comparing the two forms, we identify:

  • T(y)=yT(y) = yT(y)=y
  • η=ln⁡λ  ⟹  λ=eη\eta = \ln \lambda \implies \lambda = e^\etaη=lnλ⟹λ=eη
  • a(η)=λ=eηa(\eta) = \lambda = e^\etaa(η)=λ=eη
  • b(y)=1y!b(y) = \frac{1}{y!}b(y)=y!1​

In the context of GLMs, we assume η=θTx\eta = \theta^T xη=θTx.

(b) [3 points]

The canonical response function for this family is given by:

4. [15 points] Convexity of Generalized Linear Models

Given an exponential family distribution in the form:

p(y;η)=b(y)exp⁡(ηTT(y)−a(η))p(y; \eta) = b(y) \exp\left( \eta^T T(y) - a(\eta) \right)p(y;η)=b(y)exp(ηTT(y)−a(η))

For simplicity, we assume T(y)=yT(y) = yT(y)=y and η=θTx\eta = \theta^T xη=θTx is a scalar. Thus:

p(y;η)=b(y)exp⁡(ηy−a(η))p(y; \eta) = b(y) \exp\left( \eta y - a(\eta) \right)p(y;η)=b(y)exp(ηy−a(η))

(a) [5 points]

Using the hint that the derivative and integral can be swapped:

∂∂η∫p(y;η)dy=∫∂∂ηp(y;η)dy\frac{\partial}{\partial \eta} \int p(y; \eta) dy = \int \frac{\partial}{\partial \eta} p(y; \eta) dy∂η∂​∫p(y;η)dy=∫∂η∂​p(y;η)dy

The derivative of the density function is:

∂∂ηp(y;η)=∂∂η[b(y)exp⁡(ηy−a(η))]\frac{\partial}{\partial \eta} p(y; \eta) = \frac{\partial}{\partial \eta} \left[ b(y) \exp(\eta y - a(\eta)) \right]∂η∂​p(y;η)=∂η∂​[b(y)exp(ηy−a(η))]

=b(y)exp⁡(ηy−a(η))⋅(y−∂a(η)∂η)=p(y;η)(y−∂a(η)∂η)= b(y) \exp(\eta y - a(\eta)) \cdot \left( y - \frac{\partial a(\eta)}{\partial \eta} \right) = p(y; \eta) \left( y - \frac{\partial a(\eta)}{\partial \eta} \right)=b(y)exp(ηy−a(η))⋅(y−∂η∂a(η)​)=p(y;η)(y−∂η∂a(η)​)

Integrating both sides:

∫p(y;η)(y−∂a(η)∂η)dy=∂∂η∫p(y;η)dy\int p(y; \eta) \left( y - \frac{\partial a(\eta)}{\partial \eta} \right) dy = \frac{\partial}{\partial \eta} \int p(y; \eta) dy∫p(y;η)(y−∂η∂a(η)​)dy=∂η∂​∫p(y;η)dy

Since ∫p(y;η)dy=1\int p(y; \eta) dy = 1∫p(y;η)dy=1 (normalization constant), its derivative is 000:

∫p(y;η)ydy−∂a(η)∂η∫p(y;η)dy=0\int p(y; \eta) y dy - \frac{\partial a(\eta)}{\partial \eta} \int p(y; \eta) dy = 0∫p(y;η)ydy−∂η∂a(η)​∫p(y;η)dy=0

Thus, the expected value is:

E[Y∣X;θ]=∫p(y;η)ydy∫p(y;η)dy=∂a(η)∂η\mathbb{E}[Y | X; \theta] = \frac{\int p(y; \eta) y dy}{\int p(y; \eta) dy} = \frac{\partial a(\eta)}{\partial \eta}E[Y∣X;θ]=∫p(y;η)dy∫p(y;η)ydy​=∂η∂a(η)​

(b) [5 points]

Starting from the result in 4(a):

∂a(η)∂η=E[Y∣X;θ]=∫p(y;η)ydy\frac{\partial a(\eta)}{\partial \eta} = \mathbb{E}[Y | X; \theta] = \int p(y; \eta) y dy∂η∂a(η)​=E[Y∣X;θ]=∫p(y;η)ydy

Differentiating with respect to η\etaη again:

=∫p(y;η)(y−∂a(η)∂η)ydy=∫p(y;η)y2dy−∂a(η)∂η∫p(y;η)ydy= \int p(y; \eta) \left( y - \frac{\partial a(\eta)}{\partial \eta} \right) y dy = \int p(y; \eta) y^2 dy - \frac{\partial a(\eta)}{\partial \eta} \int p(y; \eta) y dy=∫p(y;η)(y−∂η∂a(η)​)ydy=∫p(y;η)y2dy−∂η∂a(η)​∫p(y;η)ydy

=E[Y2∣X;θ]−E[Y∣X;θ]2= \mathbb{E}[Y^2 | X; \theta] - \mathbb{E}[Y | X; \theta]^2=E[Y2∣X;θ]−E[Y∣X;θ]2

Given that Var(Y∣X;θ)=E[Y2∣X;θ]−E[Y∣X;θ]2\text{Var}(Y | X; \theta) = \mathbb{E}[Y^2 | X; \theta] - \mathbb{E}[Y | X; \theta]^2Var(Y∣X;θ)=E[Y2∣X;θ]−E[Y∣X;θ]2, we have:
